utterances: No.
However, NASA has said now that these allegations have come forward, that they're going to do an investigation and they are going to find out who, when and where.
Now, I should think that would be something they want to know.
Well, there was something else that happened this week that was also bizarre -unrelated to this.

But apparently, NASA discovered that a piece of computer, like a hardware - computer hardware that was scheduled to fly to the International Space Station next month, I guess, had some wires cut in the back.
They said a deliberate act of sabotage.
What do we know about that?
That's right.
Yesterday, NASA held what is usually a very sleepy press conference having to do with whether the shuttle is ready to fly on August 7th or not.

And suddenly, sort of out of the blue, one of the officials said and by the way, we want to confirm that there is an investigation ongoing of sabotage of a piece of computer equipment that was supposed to go to the International Space Station this time.
What they found was that two wires had been intentionally cut in this piece of equipment.
It's not a piece of equipment that's very important from a safety perspective.
It's used to transfer information from a sensor on the space station down to Earth.
And they were also very clear that this did not occur in Florida where the shuttles are prepped for launch.
It was - it happened at a subcontractor who was helping prepare this equipment.

And all they've been willing to say at this point is that, they are - that they had at least two investigations ongoing; an internal investigation and that an external law enforcement agencies involved.
It's like a kind of a tough day for NASA's (unintelligible).
It's been a - not only a tough day, it's been a tough year for NASA.
Well, we'll get to the year part later.
But, David, thanks very much for the update.
Thanks.
David Malakoff is NPR science correspondent and editor here.
And he joined me in Studio 3A.
A couple of Democratic states attorneys general say that they will use all means necessary to try to block President Trump's action.
One of them is New Mexico Attorney General Hector Balderas, who joins us now from Albuquerque.
Welcome to the program.
Thank you for having me.
And in your case, what does all means necessary mean?
Well, I do believe that there will be various states attorneys general across the country that will take legal action to defend the DREAMers.
They are as American as Melania Trump.
They have complied with rules.
And more importantly, this is a much broader debate.
Rather than immigration, this is about protecting the American dream and the Constitution.
But can't a president reverse an executive action of his predecessor provided that there's no law that's been passed by Congress to back up that presidential action?
They can provided that they don't violate due process.
A president can take any legal action in defense of the nation provided it protects Americans equally.
And more importantly, the president, I believe, needs to support immigration policy that strengthens the national security.
And I do believe the DREAMers, as Americans, do strengthen national security.
But that would be a policy decision.
The president, I should say, is leaving Congress six months to pass a law that would cover the DREAMers.
If they did pass a law that kept them in the country, wouldn't that be a stronger basis for addressing their problem than an executive order?
Well, there definitely should be some long-term solution.
Clearly the president and the Congress should take a leadership role and strengthen the clarity of the law.
But I also believe that in pursuing the rule of law, you need to pursue justice.
And there is a path for these DREAMers to have their constitutional rights protected.
And I should have said in this case an executive action, since this was not technically an executive order.
Let's say you won the argument in court that a president's executive action is binding upon his successor.
Are you prepared to say that executive orders by Donald Trump can't be reversed by his successor if a federal judge says they don't violate equal rights or another constitutional provision?
Well, I don't think there's any AGs that are proposing that the president's actions be binding on the next president.
What we're simply saying is that to create a set of rules for DREAMers and strictly change the rules without any due process could potentially violate law.
But more importantly, that there should be equal protections in how we treat the immigrant population.
And clearly the DREAMers are the best of the best.
These are individuals who were brought here as children.
They have learned English.
And more importantly, they have passed background check and comply with Homeland Security.
So these are not just your run-of-the-mill immigrants who have just somehow recently crossed the border.
These are Americans for all intents and purposes.
And we do believe that they deserve a rule of law that balances justice when enforcing.

What do you say to a critic of illegal immigration who might argue that while the DREAMers' situation is sympathetic, next would come an argument for the DREAMers' parents and for other family members?
There's no shortage of people with sympathetic stories who've done things.
The issue is really whether federal immigration law is respected.
Well, I've been absolutely a very strong advocate for deporting criminals.
I've been a strong advocate for tougher border security.
But I'm also an advocate for balancing individuals who for all intents and purposes are Americans.
And more importantly, immigration policy needs to strengthen national security and our economy.
And clearly the DREAMers are superstars in getting education and work permits.
They are Americans worth defending.
And immigration should be consistent with (unintelligible) that type of law.
But again, you would say that's a legal decision, not a policy decision, as to whether they should be permitted to remain in the country.
Yes.

What I'm saying is that as they conduct those policy decisions that they should legally be treated with the type of due process and the type of equal protection that normal Americans receive when pursuing the American dream.
New Mexico Attorney General Hector Balderas, thank you very much for talking with us today.
Thank you.
(SOUNDBITE OF MAJOR LAZER SONG, ""KNOW NO BETTER - BAD BUNNY REMIX"")
This is NPR News.
In June, Judge John Roberts was a relative newcomer to the federal bench.
By midsummer, he was preparing for a seat on the Supreme Court.
And by early October, if the president has his way, John Roberts will be the 17th Chief Justice of the United States.
This morning, President Bush nominated John Roberts to succeed Justice William Rehnquist who died over the weekend.
The Senate followed that news with an announcement that it will postpone its hearings on Roberts at least until later in the week.
We want to hear from you.
Does this promotion to chief justice change your questions about John Roberts?
Give us a call at (800) 989-TALK.

And joining us now to discuss Roberts, Rehnquist and the seat still open on the court is David Savage, Supreme Court reporter for the Los Angeles Times and author of ""Turning Right: The Making of the Rehnquist Supreme Court."" He's joined us by phone from his office in Washington, DC.
Thanks for being with us, David.
Hi, Lynn.
Now this decision to promote Roberts seemed to come about very quickly following the death of Chief Justice Rehnquist.
Yes, and I think it's actually because the White House, in the spring, wanted to choose John Roberts to replace Rehnquist.
Do you remember, then we all assumed that the chief justice would step down in June, and President Bush was ready to choose Roberts to fill Rehnquist's seat?
He seemed to be the ideal choice to be the Rehnquist successor.
He's very much like him politically, a very smart lawyer.
He could run the court even though he's a young guy.
But then, of course, it didn't work out according to plan then.
Rehnquist didn't quit.
Justice O'Connor did quit.
We all thought, and there was much speculation that the White House would sort of switch course and choose a woman.
After two or three weeks, President Bush ended up going with his first choice, which was Roberts.
And I think he did that in part because he knew that if Roberts was confirmed, which was likely, he could then elevate Roberts to the seat of chief justice.
And now, of course, the chief justice had died, so it's really sort of going back to plan A.
Yeah.
Now tell us a little bit about the chief justice's role on the court.
How big a change will this be in terms of this court if Roberts is nominated--I mean, if his nomination is confirmed?
Well, we will--I guess we'll see how effective a leader John Roberts is.
He strikes me as a person who could step in and do the job pretty well from the beginning.
He's an exceptionally smart lawyer who's been dealing with federal legal issues from the beginning.
It's always sort of hard to know how a chief justice is going to work out, because it's really a question of whether the chief has a view of the law that he can impress upon it on his colleagues.
Because one of the powers you have is you get to speak first at all the conferences and sort of say, `This is a case about this.
Here's the legal issue we need to decide.
And here's how I think it should be decided.
And then the discussion goes around the table, and the question is whether there are votes with the chief or not.

You can imagine in your office or any number of offices, there are discussions, and some people are very good, some leaders of the office are very good at sort of shaping opinions, 'cause they're persuasive, and other leaders are not.
And that's the--I think John Roberts is very qualified to step into that job.
It will be a while before we know whether he can actually lead the other justices.
Now, of course, the president is going to have to still nominate someone to take the seat of Justice Sandra Day O'Connor.
She's going to stay on the court apparently.
That's right?
Yes, although I think that's more symbolic than practical.
Everybody will talk about the fact that Justice O'Connor will be there in October, but her--she's only important if she casts the ninth and deciding vote.
And the way the Supreme Court works is your vote only counts if you're there when the decision comes out.
So to take an example, they have a big case on assisted suicide the first week in October.
Let's assume John Roberts is the chief justice, and he's there, and they don't have a replacement for Justice O'Connor, so she's there.
Now they can privately vote on the case, but that opinion probably won't come out till January or February, 'cause it's likely to be divided.
And if O'Connor is the fifth vote, they'll be stuck with a four-four split, in which case they'll dismiss the case or reargue it.
So for whatever it's worth, I think it's mostly symbolic to say that she's going to be there in October.
Well, is there a chance that they could have another Supreme Court justice on the court by October?
Possible, but very unlikely.
The president said he'd move, you know, in a timely manner.
Suppose a week from now, he chooses Alberto Gonzales or Edith Clement.
Those are the two--you know, Judge Gonzales or a woman has been considered as the likely choice now.
The Senate would then hold hearings.
And suppose it works very smoothly, I think they could be there, confirmed, in late October or early November.

So they would miss a few cases, and if the Supreme Court does split four-four, I would presume the court would just announce, `We're going to reargue the case in several months with the new justice in his or her seat,' and then they'll have a full nine-member court to decide the matter.
All right.
Well, thanks so much for joining us today, David.
(Singing) The sun goes down, another dreamless night.
You're right by my side.
Thank you, Lynn.
David Savage is a Supreme Court reporter for the Los Angeles Times and author of ""Turning Right: The Making of the Rehnquist Supreme Court."" He joined us by phone from his office in Washington, DC.
This is TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
I'm Lynn Neary.
Doctors have treated thousands of people suffering from severe depression with ketamine.
It's a hallucinogenic club drug also known as Special K, and it isn't approved for treating depression.
But as NPR's Jon Hamilton reports, more and more doctors are giving ketamine to patients who've run out of other options.
Gerard Sanacora is a psychiatrist at Yale University who has given ketamine to hundreds of patients.
As a street drug, ketamine can be dangerous, and for medical purposes, it's approved only as an anesthetic.
So Sanacora says sometimes other doctors tell him...
